danger_signals.visual_form: Various themed enemies across elemental levels (water, earth, air, fire enemies)
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates hazards to avoid or defeat; collision results in player damage/level restart

danger_signals.visual_form: Platforming obstacles themed to musical segments (e.g., Rite of Spring earth elements, Night on Bald Mountain fire hazards)
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental threats that require navigation skill; falling or collision triggers restart

danger_signals.visual_form: Scattered collectibles throughout levels that glow or stand out visually
danger_signals.meaning: Game objective markers; level incomplete without collection, implying consequence/failure state if not gathered

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible projectile items that stand out from environment
safety_signals.meaning: Player empowerment; enables offensive capability against enemies, provides temporary advantage

safety_signals.visual_form: Recognizable Disney character animation as player avatar with cartoon-like animation
safety_signals.meaning: Establishes player as protagonist in familiar, non-threatening character form; visual reassurance through brand familiarity

safety_signals.visual_form: Collected notes reconstruct soundtrack; visual feedback of progression
safety_signals.meaning: Success state indicator; demonstrates world restoration and level mastery

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mickey Mouse character sprite; Sorcerer's Apprentice costume visual
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Levels themed to classical music segments; magical aesthetic consistent across all four elemental worlds

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Enemies visually distinct per level theme (water creatures, earth beings, air entities, fire creatures)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four elemental enemy types corresponding to level themes: water (Reed Flutes, Nutcracker), earth (Rite of Spring), air (Trepak, Pastoral, Dance of Hours), fire (Night on Bald Mountain, Toccata Fugue)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemy placement and design reflects musical segment aesthetic; visual cohesion between enemy type and level world-state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Levels present with scattered, uncollected musical notes; fragmented stage visuals reflecting disorder from initial story state (Mickey asleep, notes scattered)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively present and mobile; level incompleteness reflected in missing audio/musical feedback

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visual feedback as notes are gathered; animated collection sequences; environment becomes 'complete' as musical notes accumulate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Successful enemy defeats remove threats; level progression shows diminishing enemy presence as completion nears

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Collected notes trigger visual representation of song reconstruction; music plays in full as objective completes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Transition to next elemental level; visual shift from current theme to next musical segment's aesthetic

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy contact with player sprite; collision knockback animation; potential level restart visual cue
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player forced to restart from checkpoint; enemy behavior shows aggressive pursuit or defensive stance

environmental_storytelling.example: Four distinct level worlds organized by musical composition themes and elemental associations (water, earth, air, fire); visual environment directly references source Fantasia film segments, communicating narrative progression through music-driven world design

Levels based on specific Fantasia sequences (Sorcerer's Apprentice with water, Rite of Spring with earth, Pastoral Symphony with air, Night on Bald Mountain with fire); visual aesthetics directly mirror animation style and color palettes of source material

Missing musical notes scattered throughout world; collection mechanic communicates story objective through environmental placement; visual absence of notes indicates 'damaged' world state requiring restoration
Enemies visually match level theme; water enemies in water-themed levels, fire creatures in fire levels; communicates ecological cohesion and establishes threat-to-environment relationship

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Side-scrolling platforming movement; jumping to traverse obstacles; collecting notes and magical bubbles
Can be defeated by enemy contact; forced restart from checkpoint; animated knockback reaction
Present in level space; actively mobile and responsive to player proximity; can be defeated by jumping or projectile bubble contact
Collectible projectile items; when collected, enable player attack capability against enemies; visual indication of temporary power-up state
Remain static in level environment; visually prominent; require player collection to progress; their absence indicates incomplete level state

notes: Fantasia is a licensed Disney platformer with heavy reliance on visual adaptation from the 1940 animated film. World-state communication is primarily music-driven: players restore missing musical notes across four elemental worlds, each themed to specific Fantasia film segments. The game uses minimal narrative text; instead, it communicates through visual theming (elemental level design), enemy aesthetics (enemies match environment), and collectible mechanics (notes as progress indicators). Danger is communicated through enemy presence and platforming obstacles. Safety is conveyed through magical bubbles (offensive capability) and the familiar Disney character identity. The game's poor reception noted excellent graphics and animation but criticized gameplay flow, suggesting visual communication succeeded while mechanical world-state management (collision detection, restart mechanics) created frustration. Data is qualitative (T3) due to source limitations; specific visual asset details not documented in Wikipedia article.
